coredata.dc:title: Comparison between pool boiling system of graphene quantum dots and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots suspended in binary base fluids

coredata.dc:description: Using nanofluids instead of conventional heat transfer fluids as a passive method is a well-established and widely used technique by researchers to increase the rate and thermal performance of engineering equipment. In this study, the hydrothermal method with a bottom-up approach was used for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. Then, nanofluid samples were prepared in a two-step process, by adding nanoparticles to binary base fluids of deionized water and ethylene glycol in volume concentrations of (50:50) and (60:40), in four concentrations of 100, 200, 500, and 1000 ppm. In order to better understand the boiling heat transfer mechanism and measure its characteristics such as critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient, an experimental system was designed and built. Nanofluids based on graphene quantum dots have unique features such as compatibility with the environment, economic efficiency, high stability and suitable heat transfer capability. For this reason, their selection in the pool boiling heat transfer process, in addition to saving energy, is introduced as one of the most effective options for improving CHF and HTC. The tests were performed under saturated conditions, atmospheric pressure, and on a vertical flat and polished copper thermal plate. Prepared nanofluids GQDs and N: GQDs based on DI-water maintained their apparent stability for two months. For GQDs nanofluids at an optimal concentration of 500 ppm with a volume ratio of (60:40) DI-water and EG, compared to DI-water, the greatest increase in CHF and HTC is 90.69, 85.011 % and for N: GQDs at a concentration of 500 with a volume ratio (50:50) DI-water and EG, 75.37 and 78.17 % compared to DI-water.

head.ce:title: Comparison between pool boiling system of graphene quantum dots and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots suspended in binary base fluids

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using nanofluids instead of conventional heat transfer fluids as a passive method is a well-established and widely used technique by researchers to increase the rate and thermal performance of engineering equipment. In this study, the hydrothermal method with a bottom-up approach was used for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. Then, nanofluid samples were prepared in a two-step process, by adding nanoparticles to binary base fluids of deionized water and ethylene glycol in volume concentrations of (50:50) and (60:40), in four concentrations of 100, 200, 500, and 1000 ppm. In order to better understand the boiling heat transfer mechanism and measure its characteristics such as critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient, an experimental system was designed and built. Nanofluids based on graphene quantum dots have unique features such as compatibility with the environment, economic efficiency, high stability and suitable heat transfer capability. For this reason, their selection in the pool boiling heat transfer process, in addition to saving energy, is introduced as one of the most effective options for improving CHF and HTC. The tests were performed under saturated conditions, atmospheric pressure, and on a vertical flat and polished copper thermal plate. Prepared nanofluids GQDs and N: GQDs based on DI-water maintained their apparent stability for two months. For GQDs nanofluids at an optimal concentration of 500 ppm with a volume ratio of (60:40) DI-water and EG, compared to DI-water, the greatest increase in CHF and HTC is 90.69, 85.011 % and for N: GQDs at a concentration of 500 with a volume ratio (50:50) DI-water and EG, 75.37 and 78.17 % compared to DI-water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The phenomenon of liquid to vapor phase change, which is often known as boiling heat transfer, is the most used among the three conventional methods of heat transfer (conduction, convection, and radiation) due to its high heat transfer coefficient and latent heat of evaporation [1,2]. In addition, this phenomenon is widely used in engineering and industrial facilities due to the high latent heat capacity of steam [3]. Two-(liquid–vapor) phase boiling heat transfer is widely used in all kinds of industrial heat transfer processes such as heat pumps, air conditioning, biological reactors, thermal chemical industries, cooling systems of microelectronic devices, steam boilers, and heat exchange media in nuclear and fossil fuel power plants [4–6]. Therefore, improving its efficiency is one of the most important categories of research in recent years [7]. Due to the availability of the maximum latent heat of vaporization in the nucleate pool boiling region, researchers have put their full research focus on improving heat transfer applications on this region [8,9].

The low thermal properties of conventional heat transfer fluids such as deionized water (DI-water), ethylene glycol (EG), engine oil, etc., pose a limit to the heat transfer capabilities of mechanical heat transfer equipment and therefore leads to use of larger size of heat exchangers and even leads to various other problems [1]. To overcome the limitations of such fluids researchers keep looking for ways to increase the thermal properties of fluids and meet the increasing demand for pool boiling heat transfer systems. One of the main techniques that researchers are interested in is passive methods such as modifying the boiling surface to increase nucleation sites and adding compounds such as nanoparticles and surfactants to the base fluid [7].

The addition of dense solid particles with a nominal size of 0 to 100 nm in a base fluid forms two-phase systems of liquid and solid. The solid phase is metallic or non-metallic nanoparticles, nanofibers, nanotubes, nanowires, nanorods or nanoplates, droplets of different shapes and sizes, and the liquid phase is cooling fluids such as water and engine oil [10–12]. In general, these fluid-engineered colloidal suspension systems are called nanofluids. The term nanofluid was first coined by Stephen U.S. Choi at Argonne National Lab (ANL) in 1993 [13–15]. In addition, the important parameters that determine the properties of nanofluids are: 1. Thermal fluids including temperature, density, specific heat, viscosity and enthalpy; 2. Heat transfer including thermal conductivity, heat capacity, pressure drop and Prandtl number; 3. Particles including size, shape, BET (surface area analysis) and crystalline phase; 4. Colloid including suspension stability, zeta potential and pH and 5. Lubrication including viscosity, viscosity index, wear rate, friction coefficient and extreme pressure [16].

At first, researchers widely used metallic and non-metallic nanofluids as well as oxides based on them to increase heat transfer coefficient and critical heat flux [17]. In 2016, He et al. [18] analyzed the experimental aspects of boiling heat transfer coefficient and critical heat flux of ZnO nanosuspension based on a mixture of water and ethylene glycol in a cylindrical container under atmospheric pressure. The results showed that HTC and CHF increased due to the reduction of surface wettability and the coating of nanoparticles on the surface of the heater, and nanofluids with a mass fraction less than 7.25 % can increase boiling heat transfer in 85 % volume of ethylene glycol aqueous solution. In 2017, Manetti et al. [19] experimentally evaluated the enhancement of pool boiling heat transfer using a low concentration of water-based Al2O3 nanofluid in saturated conditions. The results showed that the heat transfer coefficient of water-based Al2O3 nanofluids increased by 75 and 15 %, respectively, compared to water for smooth
R a
= 0.05
μm
and rough
R a
= 0.23
μm
surfaces. Meanwhile, adding nanoparticles to the base fluid in pool boiling heat transfer systems is effective for medium heat flux and low volume concentration. Hu et al. [20] in 2017 investigated the performance of the pool boiling process using the effect of size and concentration of SiO2 nanoparticles in a mixture of deionized water, ethylene glycol, and under real conditions. The results showed that by reducing the diameter of nanoparticles from 120 nm to 84 nm, the heat transfer coefficient increased and with the increase in concentration of nanoparticles, HTC increased rapidly and then decreased in the range of 0.25 to 1 %. Next, the researchers investigated the effect of adding hybrid nanoparticles of different metals to conventional base fluids in the pool boiling process. In 2019, Yagnem et al. [2] investigated the heat transfer process of pool boiling using Al2O3-CuO hybrid nanofluids based on deionized water in different volumetric concentrations. The results showed that the critical heat flux and thermal conductivity of composite nanofluids are always higher than single component nanofluids. The greatest increase in critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient, which was 0.01 and 0.1 %, were obtained at volume concentrations of 49.84 and 7.1 %, respectively, and the decreasing trend of heat transfer coefficient was observed with increasing volume concentration.

Further, due to environmental hazards and poor stability of nanofluids based on various metals and oxides related to them, concerns arose [21,22]. Meanwhile, surfactants were used to increase their stability. However it should be noted that the use of surfactants in high-temperature equipment causes many problems [10].

For this reason, researchers looked for carbon-based nanofluids with capabilities such as compatibility with the environment and high stability, considering the lack of physicochemical changes of various metal nanoparticles in the environment and creating many problems for humans [21,23,24]. In 2018, Akbari et al. [1] investigated experimentally the heat transfer characteristics of saturated pool boiling of aqueous nanofluid based on graphene and functionalized graphene. The results showed that with the increase in the concentration of graphene sheets, the heat transfer coefficient and critical heat flux increased, and in functionalized nanofluids, the heat transfer coefficient increased by more than 72 % at a concentration of 0.1 wt%. In addition, in all prepared concentrations, an increase in thermal conductivity and the tendency to accumulate and deposit less in functionalized graphene nanofluids compared to graphene were seen. In 2019, Akbari et al. [25] compare the effects of using aqueous nanofluids based on graphene nanoplatelets (GNP) and carbon nanotubes (CNT) in the heat transfer process of nuclear pool boiling. Preliminary results showed that CHF and HTC increased for all concentrations prepared from nanofluid samples compared to deionized water. Meanwhile, with the increase in concentration to more than 0.01 % by weight, the HTC of GNP nanofluids increased uniformly, but the HTC of CNT nanofluids decreased. According to the review of past studies, the addition of graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) and CNTs increases the critical heat flux, but the heat transfer coefficient often decreases for CNTs and increases for graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) [26–28]. In recent studies, the use of solid carbon nanoparticles in heat transfer systems has been considered due to their long-term chemical stability and suitable thermal and mechanical properties [28–30]. In Table 1 , the results of several key studies on the use of different nanofluids in the pool boiling heat transfer process are summarized.

The current novel knowledge is the first research in the field of using carbon-based quantum dots nanoparticles to better understand the pool boiling heat transfer mechanisms. In this report, the effect of adding suspended solid carbon nanostructured materials such as GQDs and N: GQDs to binary base fluids of DI-water and EG was used to investigate the heat transfer characteristics of nuclear pool boiling. Nanofluids with features such as unique thermal and mechanical properties, environmental compatibility with high solubility and stability as a passive method improve critical heat flux and boiling heat transfer coefficient.

In this section, the synthesis method of nanoparticles and the preparation steps of nanofluids related to them in the base fluids of deionized water and ethylene glycol have been explained. Then, a pool boiling heat transfer test device with special novelty and features such as a permanent sealing cover, bubble observation and its growth, surface change, and copper block thermal expansion control was designed and built.

All the chemicals required for the synthesis of nanoparticles and preparation of related nanofluids in this research are of laboratory grade and commercially available. Citric acid monohydrate (HOC(COOH)(CH2COOH)2, 99.5 % by weight), potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥85 % by weight), urea (CO(NH2)2, ≥99 % by weight), ethanol (C2H5OH, 96 % by weight) and acetone ((CH3)2CO, 99.8 % by weight) was all purchased from the company (Merck; Germany). Ethylene glycol (CH2OH)2) and deionized water as the solvent and base fluid were obtained from British and Iranian companies, respectively. Meanwhile, all materials were used without any purification. In Fig. 1 , the different stages of conducting experiments are shown.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and N: GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of graphene quantum dots nanoparticles has been done according to the hydrothermal process by the method of Sun et al. [35]. Accordingly, first 4.22 g of citric acid monohydrate as carbon source and 3.96 g of potassium hydroxide in the role of chemical activator (20:60 molar ratio), both as GQDs precursors, were added in 100 ml of deionized water [36]. Then this solution was completely dissolved using a magnetic heater stirrer for 30 min. The resulting clear solution was transferred to a PTFE Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor. This sealed autoclave was subjected to a hydrothermal process in an electric oven at 160 ℃ and for a reaction time of 4 h. The initial temperature of the electric oven when placing the autoclave was the ambient temperature and the time to reach the reaction temperature was 20 min. After completing the reaction time and cooling the autoclave body to ambient temperature naturally, the opaque white sample was removed from the autoclave. The final reaction product was first centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 30 min to neutralize the pH and remove unreacted precursors. Then by adding ethanol to it at a ratio of 1 to 1 and repeating the centrifugation operation with the previous conditions, the precipitate was collected [37]. The precipitate was dried at 105 °C for 24 h and a white powder of GQDs was obtained. The white powder of the obtained GQDs nanoparticles can be easily dispersed in deionized water. In Fig. 2 , the various steps of the synthesis of GQDs are given in full detail.

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots nanoparticles were synthesized by the method of Ettefaghi et al. [14]. According to Fig. 3 , citric acid monohydrate and urea, which are carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, were used as precursors of N: GQDs nanoparticles.

X-ray diffraction Spectroscopy (XRD) patterns to investigate the crystallinity of GQDs and N: GQDs were recorded by (X'Pert Pro, Panalytical, Netherlands) using (Cu-Kα radiation,
λ = 1.54
Å
) in a
2θ
range of 5–80° at ambient temperature. The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR, Jasco 4600, Japan) was used to analyze the functional groups in the chemical structures of the synthesized samples by Potassium bromide (KBr) pellet technique in the wavelength range of 400–4000 cm−1. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM, Quanta 200 FEG SEM, FEI, USA) with a 20 kV was used to analyze the morphology and elemental information of the synthesized nanostructures. Morphology details and particle size study of the samples were considered by high transmission electron microscope (TEM, Philips, 208S, USA) in 20–100 kV electron column and 1.3 MP CMOS camera.

The preparation of nanofluid samples is the first key step in the experimental investigation of pool boiling heat transfer with nanofluids. To prepare nanofluid samples by suspending GQD and N: GQDs nanoparticles in a mixture of deionized water and ethylene glycol as base fluids, proper mixing and stabilization of particles is required [38].

A two-step method was used to prepare nanofluid samples [39]. As shown in Fig. 4 , in the first step, a specific amount of GQDs and N: GQDs nanoparticles was slowly added to the base fluid with volume ratios of (50:50) and (60:40). This suspension was placed on the magnetic stirrer heater at ambient temperature for 7.5 min for GQDs nanoparticles and 17.5 min for N: GQDs nanoparticles. Then, to stabilize the nanofluid samples, the beginning of the nanofluid containing GQDs for 7.5 min and the nanofluid containing N: GQDs for 17.5 min were placed in the ultrasonic bath machine under a frequency of 40 kHz and a power of 200 W. At the same time, the preparation conditions of high-quality nanofluids have been optimized with items such as observation and guesswork. Nanofluid samples were prepared in four concentrations of 100, 200, 500 and 1000 ppm. In addition, due to the high solubility of synthesized nanoparticles based on DI-water and EG, there was no need to use chemical or non-chemical surfactants and pH adjustment, and in the end, no sediment was seen before the test [21,40–43].

At first, the stability of the samples was investigated using the method of apparent analysis (or static stability). So the changes in the appearance of the samples prepared in different concentrations after being transferred to a penicillin transparent bottle for 2 months without moving, were investigated by regular photography. Then, pool boiling heat transfer tests were conducted to find index samples–a sample of nanofluids prepared with optimal concentration, where nucleate pool boiling heat transfer indices such as critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient reach their maximum value after repeated tests. For this purpose, the most optimal concentration of each sample in the thermal performance test was sent for zeta potential analysis (Dynamic Stability, Horiba, SZ-100, Japan) with the measurement range and accuracy of −200 to + 200 mV and
±
2 %, respectively. The size and particle size distribution of GQDs and N: GQDs in the deionized water-based fluid were determined by the dynamic light scattering method (DLS, Horiba, SZ-100, Japan) in the range of 0.3 nm to 0.8 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pool boiling heat transfer experimental setup
ce:section.ce:para: To measure the heat transfer coefficient (HTC) and critical heat flux (CHF) of the prepared nanofluid samples, a pool boiling heat transfer experimental device was designed and built, the schematic of which is shown in Fig. 5 .

The main cylindrical boiling chamber consists of an aluminum proportional plate, a hollow PTFE Teflon rod, and borosilicate (or Pyrex) glass. The main boiling chamber is located on a PTFE Teflon rebar in the center of which a hole with a diameter of 40 mm is made to place the copper block. Pyrex glass cylinder with a height of 230 mm, an outer diameter of 100 mm, and a wall thickness of 2.5 mm is installed in the device to ensure a proper view of the nucleate-boiling stages of the working fluid. On the aluminum plate, there are two valves for charging the working fluid and installing the reflux spiral glass condenser. The water inlet of the glass condenser is from the bottom and is used to condense the vapors and prevent the reduction of the volume of the boiling working fluid.

A 1000 W stainless steel tubular heating element with a diameter of 6 mm and a height of 400 mm is inserted vertically from the top of the aluminum plate to preheat the working fluid to the saturation boiling point temperature. K-type thermocouple made of chrome and aluminum metal wire with sheath and support length of 20.25 and 17 mm respectively and outer diameter of 4 mm was used to detect the temperature of nanofluid with high accuracy [44]. To display, stabilize, and control the temperature of the working fluid, a digital thermostat (autonics, TCN4M-22R, South Korea) and a solid-state relay (autonics, SPA6015D, South Korea) have been used, which are connected to the thermocouple and the element, respectively. On the body of the copper block, 5 holes were made at different distances to place k-type thermocouples, which are connected in series to the data acquisition system (RS485 MODBUS RTU, JS201902, China) and the laptop and show the process of temperature changes during the nuclear boiling process. At the end and the bottom surface of the cylindrical copper block, four holes with a diameter of 12.1 mm and a depth of 50 mm were made to install four cartridge heating elements with a diameter of 12 mm and a length of 50 mm. Cartridge heating elements made of stainless steel, each with a power of 200 W and a total of 800 W, are used to ensure uniform heating of the copper block and the test liquid. The cartridge heaters with high-temperature resistance are first fed by the AC/DC switched-mode power supply (S-500–24, 15A, China), and then they are connected to the autotransformer Industrial voltage variable (OMEGA, TDGC2-3Kva, 12A, South Korea) of the automatic transformer to control the produced heat flux.

This set was placed on four steel springs with an external diameter of 25 mm, a height of 125 mm, and a thickness of 6 mm. These springs, designed according to the weight of the whole assembly, were used in the lowest part of the device to neutralize the longitudinal thermal expansion of the copper block and not reduce the efficiency of the device during the test [45].

Due to its characteristics such as low thermal expansion coefficient, high thermal conductivity, corrosion resistance, cheapness, and availability, copper metal has many applications in industrial heat transfer equipment.

Therefore, in this research, the solid copper cylindrical shaft has been used as a solid phase in order to accelerate the transformation of the liquid phase into steam in the pool boiling heat transfer phenomenon. The pool boiling test was performed on a copper block (high grade, by Percent purity ≥ 99 %, Iran) with a diameter of 40 mm and height of 110 mm with a flat surface. Due to the more uniform coating of nanoparticles, the possibility of easy measurement of surface properties such as coating thickness, and better observation of their effect on heat transfer properties with greater accuracy, a copper block with a flat surface without grooves has been used.

According to Fig. 6 .a, the distance of thermocouples No. 1, 2, and 3 in the radial axis from the welding surface is 14 mm, and the distance of thermocouples No. 4 and 5 in the longitudinal axis of thermocouple No. 3 is 15 and 30 mm, respectively. Meanwhile, the distance of thermocouple number 5 in the longitudinal axis to the end of the copper block is 66 mm. According to Fig. 6.b, on the right and left sides of the cylindrical copper block, two holes with a diameter of 3 mm and a depth of 10 mm are installed to place two thermocouples type k with an external diameter of 2 mm and a support length of 12 mm. Also, in the center and on the longitudinal axis of the cylindrical copper block, three holes with a diameter of 3 mm and a depth of 20 mm are installed in the direction of installing three k-type thermocouples with an external diameter of 2 mm and a support length of 20 mm. According to Fig. 7 , fixed sealing O-rings (Fluorocarbon, Viton, England) were placed on the cylindrical copper block at different distances.

Due to the fact that the characteristics of the boiling surface have important effects on the boiling properties, for the conditions to be the same in all experiments, before each experiment, the test glass chamber is first thoroughly washed and dried with deionized water. Then, the boiling surface is polished using ultra-soft stainless sandpaper No. 1000 and then No. 2500 for 2 min each [46]. To be precise, first the copper cylindrical shaft was connected to the heavy duty bench vise, and then after the sandpapers were fully connected to the polishing machine, completely smooth and polished surfaces were provided to start the tests.

After that, the boiling chamber is first with acetone and then with deionized water, thoroughly washed, cleaned, and dried. In each experiment, 1000 ml of nanofluid mass is charged [27]. The spiral tube electric heater, which was already inside the main boiling chamber, is turned on to remove excess gases and quickly bring the temperature of the nanofluid solution to the saturation temperature. Thus, in the air pressure of the laboratory environment, first, the nanofluid boils and then the nanofluid charging valve screw is opened and 10 min is given for the excess gases inside the nanofluid to escape and then the nanofluid charging valve screw is closed again. At this stage, the stainless steel cartridge heaters enter the circuit one after the other and the input voltage to them is adjusted and controlled by the voltage controller. Each time the voltage changes to a constant value and reaches a temperature equilibrium after 10 min, the voltage across two ends of the electrical winding the variable voltage autotransformer increases, and a constant current is added to the current passing through the circuit, and temperatures are recorded. The increase in voltage continues until the critical boiling point is reached, and at this moment, when the boiling device takes on an explosive state, the tests end. Also, the trend of temperature changes in different parts of the copper block, which was given to the display by k-type thermocouples, are analyzed to calculate the critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient.

Due to the high grade of the copper block and the use of Teflon PTFE rebar around it, as well as the high thermal conductivity of the copper metal compared to Teflon, the thermal drop in the copper block in the radius during the pool boiling process was ignored. Therefore, the heat transfer flux on the boiling surface was calculated in the axial direction and temperature of the boiling surface following the one-dimensional thermal conductivity equation, as shown in the eq. (1) [7,19,31].

In Fig. 8 , the X-ray energy diffraction patterns that characterize the structure of GQDs and N: GQDs nanoparticle samples are given. X-ray energy diffraction pattern of GQD and N: GQD nanoparticles, respectively with the characteristic peak
2 θ = 24.275
and
2 θ = 26.925
with a Layer d-Spacing of about 208.15 °A and 187.852 °A which indicates the hydrothermal process followed by oxidation, approximately It was similar to the graphite pattern [35].

The Fourier transform infrared spectrum of GQDs and N: GQDs nanoparticles samples to identify surface functional groups is shown in Fig. 9 . Regarding the sample of GQDs nanoparticles, the broad absorption peak observed in the region of 2750–3500 cm−1, indicates the presence of a large number of hydroxyl groups
( O - H )
on the surface of the GQDs nanoparticles sample, which shows the hydrophilic property of this sample, whose exact absorption peak is it 3176.19 cm−1. Meanwhile, the stretching vibration peak
( C - H )
can be seen in region 3033.4 cm−1 and the stretching absorption peak (bending vibrations)
( C = H )
can be seen in region 1556.27 cm−1 [35].

In the case of the N: GQDs nanoparticles sample, a broad absorption peak (broad absorption band) was observed in the region of 2900–3500 cm−1, which indicates the presence of a large number of hydroxyl
( O - H )
and amino
N - H
groups on the surface of the N: GQDs sample, which The result shows the high hydrophilic property in this sample. The absorption peak
( O - H )
of 3278.39 cm−1 and the stretching vibration absorption peak
( N - H )
overlapped in this region so that the peak related to NH2 can be seen in 2975.69 cm−1. The stretching vibration peak
( C - H )
appeared in region 2938.98 cm−1. In addition, the peak observed in 1577.49 cm−1 was due to the stretching absorption related to
( C = C )
, which indicates the basic structure of graphene. The results of the FTIR spectrum of the N: GQDs sample show the presence of functional groups in its structure, which leads to the solubility of the N: GQDs sample in water [21].

The average size of GQD and N: GQDs nanoparticles can be seen from the results of the dynamic light scattering study in Fig. 10 , and this very small size is between 1–4 nm, which adds to the stability of the created nanofluids [1,21].

The FE-SEM images of GQDs and N: GQDs nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 11 . Also, the FE-SEM images show that the nanoparticles in question have a homogeneous, consistent, and dot-shaped morphology, and the crystalline behavior of GQDs nanoparticles can be seen. Meanwhile, the formation of aggregate particles in the N-GQD sample probably indicates the aggregation of graphene surfaces [49,50].

According to Fig. 12 , to better investigate the structure and determine the exact size of GQDs and N: GQDs nanoparticles in different magnifications, a transmission electron microscope has been used. The results of TEM images show that the particles have lattice, dot, and graphite-like morphology and these characteristics are due to the crystalline behavior of their layers. Meanwhile, both synthesized nanoparticles are well dispersed in a narrow size distribution with an average lateral size of less than 5 nm and around 3 nm. At the same time, the above results show that the above nanoparticles may be composed of nanocrystalline cores of graphite SP2 carbon atoms [1,29,35,51]. In addition, the reason for the direct relationship between the morphology of graphene-based quantum dot nanoparticles and the increase in heat transfer in thermal systems, especially pool boiling, is the following. 1. Enhanced Surface Area: hierarchical or porous structures can provide a larger surface area compared to conventional nanoparticles. This increased surface area allows more active sites for nucleation during the boiling process and more effectively promotes bubble formation and separation; 2. Facilitated Bubble Nucleation: unique structures, such as rough surfaces, can favor nucleation sites for bubble formation and growth, enabling more efficient heat transfer by enhancing bubble dynamics; 3. Improved Wettability: increased surface wettability and better liquid distribution during the boiling process ensure uniform distribution and faster bubble release; 4. Special morphology: enabling increased local heating and concentrating heat transfer on the surface at critical points, accelerates evaporation and increases conduction, and efficiency increases continuously during the process; 5. Controlled Bubble Dynamics: easy adjustment of morphology helps to control the dynamics of bubbles during the process and by influencing factors such as bubble size, frequency of bubble separation and heat transfer mechanism, optimizes the boiling process; 6. Increased Surface Roughness: irregularity on the surface increases the wetting behavior of the boiling liquid, and further, with the increase in surface roughness, factors such as the contact surface between the liquid and the boiling surface, the nucleation and dynamics of bubbles increase, and finally, it creates efficient heat transfer; 7. Surface Energy Modification: changing the characteristics of the surface energy causes better liquid distribution and interaction with the solid surface, and finally, with a direct effect on the wetting and nucleation of bubbles, it increases the heat transfer performance [52–54].

First, nanofluid samples GQDs and N: GQDs were prepared based on deionized water in four concentrations of 100, 200, 500, and 1000 ppm, and after transferring to transparent glass containers, its apparent stability was investigated. Samples were photographed regularly in a two-month interval. According to Fig. 13 , the samples prepared without the use of surfactant and pH change maintained their excellent initial stability, and no sediment was observed in the glass containers. Of course, slight changes in the color of the samples were seen during different times.

Therefore, the observation and analysis of any subtle color change in the samples, in addition to being a useful indicator for stability or instability such as the accumulation and sedimentation of particles in the base fluid, can directly affect the performance and properties of nanofluids during the pool boiling process.

In addition, due to the low concentration and high stability of nanofluids, the rheological properties of base fluids do not change significantly, thus preventing damage and malfunction of mechanical systems [55]. Meanwhile, the main reason for the long-term stability of colloidal suspensions based on GQD and N: GQD nanoparticles are factors such as the following. 1. Surface Functionalization: creating a spatial barrier and preventing aggregation due to the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl functional groups; 2. Electronic Structure: quantum confinement effects and edge effects with minimization of energy states; 3. Dispersion in different solvents: having surface functional groups; 4. Size and shape control: non-aggregation in quantum nanoparticles intrinsically; 5. Passivation: protection of the surface in order not to degrade in certain molecules and ligands; 6. Thermal stability: non-degradation of structure and properties at high temperatures of small dot nanoparticles [56–58].

After performing pool boiling heat transfer tests, the maximum increase in critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient among the prepared samples was obtained at the optimal concentration of 500 ppm. Then, to compare the stability of nanofluids prepared by the appearance method, a concentration of 500 ppm of each of the deionized water-based nanofluids was selected for zeta potential analysis as the optimal concentration.

According to Fig. 14 , the results for the GQD and N: GQDs colloidal nanofluid samples based on deionized water at a concentration of 500 ppm show peak values of −30.4 and –33.8 mV, respectively. According to the obtained results, the high peak of the zeta potential analysis, regardless of its positive and negative sign, increases the electrostatic repulsion force and shows the high physical stability of the system. In addition, the stability and solubility of graphene quantum dots nanoparticles functionalized with nitrogen is higher than graphene quantum dots nanoparticles, which is consistent with the laboratory results of Shazali et al. [30] in 2018. The difference between the peaks and the negative value of both samples is related to the difference in pH and functional groups on the surface, respectively.

. Due to the effects of buoyancy force and latent heat, the boiling heat transfer coefficient is much higher than the displacement heat transfer coefficient without phase change. The appearance of surface tension comes from the important effect of this fluid property on bubble formation and growth. The values of surface tension and latent heat of vaporization of water are available from the thermodynamic reference table. Meanwhile, Rohsenow’s correlation is only valid for clean surfaces and does not depend on the direction and size of the heater.

To evaluate the reliability and accuracy of the experimental device, nucleate pool boiling heat transfer tests were first performed with deionized water, which was selected as the validation fluid and then the trend of experimental data changes, due to the importance of reproducibility, has been performed several times with the same conditions, was compared with the data predicted by the Rohsenow’s well-known correlation. According to Fig. 15 , the results of experimental pool boiling heat transfer data for deionized water are well matched with Rohsenow prediction and show the uniformly stability of boiling heat transfer basic properties such as the roughness of the boiling surface and the saturation temperature and pressure in the next time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Boiling curves for GQDs and N: GQDs/DI-water: EG (50:50) nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para:
The boiling curve of experimental testing of nanofluids of GQDs and N: GQDs based on DI-water and EG in a volume ratio (50:50) is depicted in Figs. 16 and 17
, respectively. The findings show that, at the same wall superheat temperature, the critical heat flux, and heat transfer coefficient in comparison to deionized water rises with an increase in nanofluids concentration up to 500 ppm and subsequently declines with an increase in concentration up to 1000 ppm. Table 2 shows the quantity and percentage of changes in the graphene quantum dots nanoparticles' heat transfer properties in the base fluid of deionized water and ethylene glycol by volume (50:50).

The maximum critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient for deionized water base fluid are 869.16 Kw/m2 and 35.96 Kw/m2.°C, respectively. Also, the maximum value of the critical heat flux and the heat transfer coefficient for the base fluid of deionized water and ethylene glycol in volume ratio (50:50) were obtained as 932.33 Kw/m2 and 37.93 Kw/m2. °C, respectively. According to Table 2, in the most optimal case, an increase of approximately 75.37 and 78.17 % for heat flux and heat transfer coefficient, respectively, compared to deionized water and an increase of 63.48 and 68.91 %, compared to the base fluid of water and ethylene glycol in volume ratio (50:50) was seen for N: GQDs nanofluids at a concentration of 500.

At the beginning of the conducted experiments, the thickness of the coating layer of nanoparticles on the hot surface has a minimum value due to reasons such as the polished surface of the boiling, gravity, natural displacement and electric field. Therefore, the contact angle between the surface and the fluid is almost the same. And because most of the thickness of the nanoparticle layer on the hot surface is done during the boiling process, with the continuation of the boiling process of nanofluids at a concentration of 100 ppm, the contact angle is slightly higher than the polished surface. As a result, the critical heat flux and the heat transfer coefficient decrease with the reduction of surface wettability in some cases, and these observed findings are consistent with the results of Kwark et al. [61] in 2010.

By increasing the concentration from 100 to 200 ppm, the wettability of the surface increases as one of the influencing factors in increasing the heat transfer characteristics, and the contact angle between the bubble and the surface is less than 90°, so the heat transfer coefficient is increasing. Meanwhile, the nanofluids prepared at 100 and 200 ppm are not deposited enough on the hot surface to fill the active sites of nucleation, so the process of increasing the heat flux with increasing concentration at low concentrations is not affected, and these results are also by Kim et al. was observed in 2007 [62]. The competition between the factor of filling large pores in the low concentration of nanoparticles in the base fluid, the reduction of active nucleation sites, and the factor of thin deposit layer on the hot surface in the continuation of the boiling process was expressed by Narayan et al. [63] in 2007.

At low concentrations, the large pores on the surface are active, and by increasing the concentration from 200 to 500 ppm, the small pores on the surface are activated and increase the heat flux. However, at a concentration of 500 ppm, a coating of nanoparticles of GQDs and N: GQDs is created on the hot surface and reduces the active sites of nucleation. However the small vapor bubbles are produced separately and due to the reduction of active nucleation sites, they collide with each other less and reduce the cohesion and formation of larger bubbles. Therefore, the coating of the surface with a layer of steam will be delayed and the critical heat flux will increase. The surface coating, by a thin layer of GQDs and N: GQDs nanoparticles, traps larger amounts of liquid phase fluid near the hot surface. Therefore, this porous morphology of the surface increases the wettability of the surface and thus causes a potential increase in the critical heat flux at the optimal concentration, which is consistent with the experimental observations of Bang et al. [64] in 2005 and Kim et al. [42] in 2007. However, with the continuation of the boiling process on the hot surface, due to the filling of the cavities with GQDs and N: GQDs nanoparticles, a decrease in the active nucleation sites and as a result a decrease in the critical heat flux is observed. Therefore, creating the thermal resistance of thin layers of sediment has caused a decrease in heat transfer characteristics and these results are consistent with the laboratory findings of You et al. in 2003, Bang et al. [64] in 2005, and Kwark [61] et al. [65] in 2010.

According to the results obtained from the contact angle test in the nanofluid sample prepared in a volume ratio of (50:50) deionized water and ethylene glycol, the higher wettability of the N: GQDs-based nanofluid sample compared to GQDs has been clearly shown. Therefore, it can be concluded that the reason for the significant increase in the critical heat flux and the heat transfer coefficient at the optimal concentration of 500 ppm was the low contact angle. In general, the surface of copper in these conditions is relatively hydrophilic, rough and irregular, which can be very close to Quan et al. [67] research in 2017.

With the increase in concentration from 500 to 1000 ppm, the main reason for the decrease in nanofluid heat transfer coefficient and copper hot surface conductivity is factors such as increasing the rate of formation, growth, and thickness of the sediment layer on the surface, and this factor is quite noticeable. So, with the inclination of the deposit structure from nano to microlayer, it is practically impossible to separate the bubbles and this phenomenon increases the tolerance of the surface against the critical heat flux. These results were confirmed by the experimental findings of Chen et al. in 2017. So the creation of a sedimentary porous surface and its thickening over time and the present results were confirmed with the experimental findings of Chen et al. [68] in 2017. They found that increasing the concentration of nanofluids up to the optimal concentration of 0.003 % by volume and continuing to increase the concentration trend after the optimal concentration leads to an increase and a decrease in the heat transfer characteristics, respectively, compared to the water fluid. The phenomenon of nanoparticle coating on the surface during the boiling process and its direct effect on heat transfer characteristics were discussed by various researchers [63,69–72]. As Kim et al. [42] in 2007, found that the type and concentration of nanoparticles caused the change in the microscopic shape of the surface and may give it different physical properties. Also, Vassallo et al. [73] in 2004 concluded that the use of SiO2 nanoparticles causes roughness on the boiling surface and this coating increases with increasing concentration. However, the use of Al2O3 nanoparticles creates a softer coating on the surface and the thickness of the sediment layer formed on it varies locally and its coating does not change with increasing concentration. According to the obtained results, in all experiments, the concentration of 500 ppm is considered the optimal concentration, which was confirmed in 2017 by Abdollahi et al [74] and many other researchers [65,75–77].

By increasing the concentration from 500 to 1000 ppm, the main reason for the decrease in the heat transfer coefficient cannot be the decrease in the thermal conductivity coefficient of the hot surface. Because the deposit layer, although it becomes thicker with increasing concentration, slowly decreases the conductivity coefficient of the surface. Therefore, the creation of a nanoparticle deposit layer inside the working fluid on the hot surface delays the activation of the nucleation sites on the hot surface and reduces the number of nucleation sites, and these two factors are the main reason for the reduction of the transfer coefficient at the concentration of 1000 ppm. So, Park et al. [28] in 2009 and Kim et al. [42,62] in 2007 mentioned the reduction of the number of nucleation sites as a factor in reducing the pool boiling heat transfer coefficient. Of course, this argument seems correct according to the results, because the grooves and pores on the hot surface, which are considered the nucleation sites of the boiling of the base fluid, are the first points on which the thin layer of nanoparticles is formed [28,42].

The highest increase in boiling heat transfer characteristics compared to deionized water has been reported for graphene quantum dots nanoparticles functionalized with nitrogen, at a concentration of 500 ppm and a volume ratio of (50:50) nanofluids. Due to the greater stability and solubility of functionalized nanofluids, the physical and thermal properties of base fluids such as viscosity and thermal conductivity have increased and as a result increased the critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient, which is consistent with the laboratory data of Akbari et al. in 2018 [1].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Boiling curves for GQDs and N: GQDs/DI-water: EG (60:40) nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para:
The boiling curve of experimental tests of nanofluids of GQDs and N: GQDs based on deionized water and ethylene glycol in volume ratio (60:40) is depicted in Figs. 18 and 19
, respectively. The findings show that at the same wall superheat temperature, the heat flow and heat transfer coefficient in comparison to deionized water rises with an increase in nanoparticle concentration up to 500 ppm and subsequently falls with an increase in concentration up to 1000 ppm. Table. 4 shows changes in heat flow and heat transfer coefficient at all concentrations.

Table 5 shows, in two separate sections, the actual surface of copper after pool boiling tests of nanofluids in a volume ratio of 60:40 of deionized water and ethylene glycol and the contact angle of DI-water drops on the deposited surfaces at room temperature and standard atmospheric conditions. At a concentration of 500 ppm with a volume ratio of (60:40) of deionized water and ethylene glycol, the deposition surface with nanofluid based on GQDs shows the lowest contact angle, contrary to expectation. Therefore, the greatest improvement for critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient occurs in these conditions. In order to justify the increase in the thermal characteristics of GQDs-based nanofluids compared to N: GQDs, we can mention the thermophysical property of copper and fluid surface, which researchers seek to optimize [78–80]. As a result, if the sediment layers are regular, smooth, thick and have strong hydrophilicity, due to the active nanofluids that have a lot of solubility, the opposite results are observed in the heat transfer characteristics of pool boiling. In addition, the details of the results obtained from static contact angle tests for nanofluids prepared in a (60:40) volume ratio of deionized water/ethylene glycol mixture are given in Table S2.

Fig. 20 shows the effect of hydrodynamic behavior between the boiling surface and bulk of different nanofluids. In fact, the development of auxiliary capillary flows causes the deposition of nanoparticles on the boiling surface and thus helps the heat transfer [81]. So, the creation of dry patches/hot spots is delayed and bubble formation increases, for this reason, with the increase in the extent of operating limits, the heat transfer characteristics of pool boiling, such as critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient, increase [9,66,82].

Referring to Table 4, the most optimal possible mode of using binary nanofluids of DI-water and EG at a volume ratio of (60:40) is seen, unlike the previous case, for GQDs nanofluids with a volume ratio of 500 ppm. So the highest increase in critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient compared to DI-water and binary mixture of DI-water and EG were obtained by 90.69, 85.011, 7.68 and 69.84 % respectively.

According to the results obtained from the tests, adding ethylene glycol to the deionized water base fluid at a ratio of (50:50) and then changing this volume ratio to (60:40) in both nanofluids based on GQDs and N: GQDs, improves the heat transfer properties of boiling.

So the experiments of Nabil et al. [83] in 2017 justified the changes in the base fluid and its direct relationship with the heat transfer characteristics. According to his results, in a constant wall superheat temperature, with an increase in concentration from 0.03 to 0.05 % by volume and with an increase in the volume ratio from (50:50) to (60:40) of water and ethylene glycol, the maximum critical heat flux and with the increase of concentration from 0.05 to 0.1 % by volume in all superheat wall temperatures, the reduction of critical heat flux has been noticeable.

The results of Tables 2 and 4 show the effect of functional groups on boiling heat transfer characteristics. By increasing the volume ratio of DI-water: EG to (60:40), nanofluid GQDs compared to N: GQDs showed better results, which was a little unpredictable. In fact, in the volume ratio of (60:40), the critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient are more affected by surface roughness than thermal conductivity and viscosity. Therefore, the competition between the solubility and stability of nanofluids in long-term tests has caused changes in the surface morphology, which directly affects the dynamics of vapor bubbles and the boiling heat transfer mechanism and these results are consistent with the laboratory data of Mehralizadeh et al. in 2020 and Hamid et al. [84] in 2018.

According to Tables 2 and 4, at low concentrations of N: GQDs nanofluids in volume ratio (50:50) and (60:40) of DI-water and EG, a slight decrease in critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient can be seen, and with increasing concentration, decrease the critical heat flux and the heat transfer coefficient are not evident and an increasing trend is seen with increasing concentration in the heat transfer characteristics. Meanwhile, at low concentrations of nanofluids, only large pores are active on the surface and small pores do not play a role. With the continuation of the boiling process at low concentration, some of these cavities are filled and the active sites of nucleation are reduced, and the reduction of heat transfer characteristics is the reason. But with the continuation of the boiling process at the concentration of 500 ppm, the number of small and large bubbles increases and in addition to increasing the number of holes, there is not enough time to fill the holes and the heat transfer characteristics reach their maximum value. By increasing the concentration to 1000 ppm, many small cavities are activated and filled by nanoparticles and reduce the active sites of nucleation, which causes a decrease in the critical heat flux and the heat transfer coefficient of prepared nanofluids. This decrease in heat transfer characteristics in high concentrations of different nanofluids was observed by the laboratory results of Das et al. [70] in 2003 and Liu et al. [71] in 2008.

In this experimental study, the potential effect of nanofluids as a passive method in improving heat transfer was investigated. Among the investigated characteristics of heat transfer are critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient. The following important results were obtained from the present study:
•
The results of the analysis of the characteristics for both synthesized samples show a crystalline structure with a size of less than 10 nm.
•
The factors of type and size of nanoparticles, concentration, and stability of nanofluids have a direct effect on the heat transfer properties of pool boiling.
•
The critical heat flux and boiling heat transfer coefficient of nanofluids were significantly higher than the base fluid of deionized water.
•
At a concentration of 100 ppm, the competition between the factor of filling large holes, reducing the active nucleation sites, and creating a thin layer of sediment on the hot surface with the continuation of the boiling process is effective in reducing the thermal properties. The maximum critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient were also attained after increasing the concentration of nanofluids in both samples to the ideal concentration of 500 ppm. The trend of decreasing heat flux and heat transfer coefficient was seen by increasing the concentration from 500 to 1000 ppm. Continuing the boiling process at a concentration of 1000 will disrupt the boiling process as a whole due to the creation of a thick coating.
•
Adding non-metallic impurities such as nitrogen to graphene quantum dot nanoparticles can increase the pool boiling heat transfer properties. Nanofluids N: GQDs with a volume ratio of (50:50) DI-water and EG increase the boiling properties more than nanofluids GQDs with the same volume ratio.
•
It was found that increasing the volume ratio of deionized water to EG from (50:50) to (60:40) increases all the heat transfer characteristics of samples GQDs and N: GQDs. The positive effect of increasing the volume ratio of base fluids from (50:50) to (60:40) for GQDs-based nanofluids in the characteristics of boiling heat transfer is greater.
•
The highest increase in critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient in GQDs nanofluids based on volume concentration of (60:40) DI-water to EG compare to the DI-water was reported to be approximately 90.69 and 85.011 %. The highest increase in critical heat flux and heat transfer coefficient in N: GQDs nanofluids based on volume concentration of (60:40) DI-water to EG compare to the DI-water was reported to be approximately 70.29 and 78.25 %.
•
The analysis to determine the apparent stability of nanofluid samples based on GQDs and N: GQDs has not shown any sediment in two months. The analysis of zeta potential has shown the stability of GQDs-based nanofluids more than N: GQDs-based nanofluids. In the stability of nanofluids, factors such as the type, size, and functional groups of nanoparticles, the solubility of nanoparticles in the base fluid, and the volume ratio of nanofluids are effective.
